Installing Node.js with NVM on Windows

5 min read
Just now

--

Stop reinstalling Node.js for every project. Learn how to manage multiple Node.js versions effortlessly with NVM for Windows.

Introduction
If you’re a JavaScript developer, you’ve probably encountered this situation:

• One project requires Node.js 20

• Another requires Node.js 22

• A new framework recommends the latest version

• An older project breaks after you update Node.js
Managing Node.js versions manually quickly becomes frustrating.
Fortunately, there’s a better solution: NVM (Node Version Manager).
NVM allows you to install, switch, and manage multiple Node.js versions on the same machine without conflicts. Whether you’re a beginner learning JavaScript or a professional working across multiple projects, NVM is one of the first tools you should install.
In this guide, we’ll walk through everything you need to know — from installation to best practices.

What is NVM?
NVM (Node Version Manager) is a version management tool that allows you to install multiple versions of Node.js and switch between them with a single command.
Instead of uninstalling one version and installing another, NVM handles everything for you.
For Windows users, we’ll use NVM for Windows, a native implementation designed specifically for Windows.

Why Should You Use NVM?
Imagine working on three different projects:
ProjectRequired Node VersionLegacy API18.xReact Application20.xNew Next.js Project22.x
Without NVM, switching between these versions means repeatedly uninstalling and reinstalling Node.js.
With NVM, switching takes just one command:
nvm use 22.18.0That’s it.
Benefits of using NVM

• ✅ Install multiple Node.js versions

• ✅ Switch versions instantly

• ✅ Keep projects isolated

• ✅ Test applications on different versions

• ✅ Avoid version conflicts

• ✅ Improve development workflow

Prerequisites
Before getting started, make sure you have:

• Windows 10 or Windows 11

• Administrator access

• Internet connection
If Node.js is already installed manually, it’s recommended to remove it first.

Step 1: Uninstall Existing Node.js
If you’ve previously installed Node.js directly from the official website:

• Open Settings

• Navigate to Apps

• Search for Node.js

• Click Uninstall

• Restart your computer
This prevents conflicts between the standalone installation and NVM.

Step 2: Install NVM for Windows
Visit the official GitHub repository:
https://github.com/coreybutler/nvm-windows/releases
Download the latest installer:
nvm-setup.exeRun the installer and follow the setup wizard.
The default installation settings work well for most developers, so there’s usually no need to change them.
After installation, restart your terminal.

Step 3: Verify the Installation
Open Windows Terminal, PowerShell, or Command Prompt and run:
nvm versionIf everything is installed correctly, you’ll see something like:
1.2.2Congratulations! NVM is now ready to use.

Step 4: View Available Node.js Versions
To see all available Node.js versions:
nvm list availableExample output:
18.20.8
20.19.5 (LTS)
22.18.0 (LTS)
24.6.0Tip: Unless your project specifies otherwise, choose the latest LTS (Long-Term Support) version.

Step 5: Install Node.js
Install Node.js 22:
nvm install 22.18.0Or install another version:
nvm install 20.19.5NVM downloads and installs Node.js automatically.

Step 6: Start Using the Installed Version
Installing a version doesn’t automatically activate it.
Use:
nvm use 22.18.0Expected output:
Now using node v22.18.0 (64-bit)
Step 7: Verify Node.js and npm
Check the installed versions:
node -vExample:
v22.18.0Now check npm:
npm -vExample:
10.9.3Your development environment is now ready.

Your First Node.js Project
Create a project directory:
mkdir my-node-app
cd my-node-appInitialize npm:
npm init -yCreate a file named index.js:
console.log("Hello, Node.js!");Run the program:
node index.jsOutput:
Hello, Node.js!Success!

Switching Between Node.js Versions
One of NVM’s biggest advantages is how easy it is to switch versions.
Install another version:
nvm install 20.19.5Switch to it:
nvm use 20.19.5Switch back later:
nvm use 22.18.0Verify:
node -v
List Installed Versions
To see every installed Node.js version:
nvm listExample:
* 22.18.0
20.19.5The asterisk (*) indicates the currently active version.

Remove an Unused Version
If you no longer need a version:
nvm uninstall 20.19.5Only that version will be removed.

Most Useful NVM Commands
CommandPurposenvm versionShow NVM versionnvm listList installed Node.js versionsnvm list availableShow downloadable versionsnvm install <version>Install a Node.js versionnvm use <version>Switch Node.js versionnvm uninstall <version>Remove a versionnode -vShow current Node.js versionnpm -vShow npm version

Common Problems (and How to Fix Them)
nvm is not recognized
If Windows doesn’t recognize the command:

• Restart your terminal.

• Restart your computer.

• Verify that NVM was installed successfully.

• Ensure the installation completed without errors.

Node Version Doesn’t Change
If node -v still shows the old version:
You likely still have a standalone Node.js installation.
Uninstall it from Settings → Apps, then run:
nvm use 22.18.0
npm Isn’t Working
Simply activate the version again:
nvm use 22.18.0Then verify:
npm -v
Best Practices
Here are a few habits that will save you time:

• Use the latest LTS version for production work.

• Avoid installing Node.js directly after setting up NVM.

• Switch versions only when required by a project.

• Read project documentation to identify the required Node.js version.

• Keep NVM updated.

Frequently Asked Questions
Can I install multiple Node.js versions?
Yes. That’s exactly what NVM is designed for.

Does each Node version have its own npm?
Yes.
Every installed Node.js version comes bundled with its own compatible npm version.

Can different projects use different Node.js versions?
Absolutely.
Simply switch versions before working on a project:
nvm use <version>
Should I always use the latest version?
Not necessarily.
For most production projects, the latest LTS release offers the best balance of stability and long-term support.

Final Thoughts
If you’re serious about Node.js development, NVM should be part of your default setup.
It eliminates the hassle of managing multiple Node.js installations, makes switching between projects effortless, and ensures your development environment stays clean and reliable.
Whether you’re building personal projects, contributing to open source, or working in a professional team, using NVM will save you time and prevent version-related headaches.
The next time a project asks for a different Node.js version, you won’t need to reinstall anything — you’ll simply switch versions with a single command.
nvm use <version>Happy coding!

If you found this guide helpful, consider bookmarking it for future reference or sharing it with fellow developers who are just getting started with Node.js.
